aliases: Croissant sandwich with ham and egg

english: Croissant sandwich with ham and egg

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific 'ham and egg croissant' entry; closest defensible match is croissant sandwich food product (FOODON_00002473).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP covers sandwiches and filled rolls; no specific ham-and-egg croissant code exists. This is the closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for ham and egg croissant sandwich; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical ham and egg croissant sandwich weighs approximately 130 g, comprising a full-sized butter croissant, one slice of picnic ham, and one fried egg.

nutrient_source.title: Ham and Egg Croissant — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Ham and Egg Croissant (cr_id F08020035). All per-100g values are taken as reported. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central croissant and ham composite data as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this item; see notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for ham and egg croissant. GI of 67 is estimated using the University of Sydney GI Database value for plain croissant (GI ~67, medium category, glucose reference). The ham and egg filling contributes protein and fat which may modestly lower the effective GI, but the croissant base dominates the carbohydrate load. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (67 × 22.78 × 1.30) / 100 = 19.8 per 130 g serving. Carbohydrate per serving = 22.78 g/100g × 1.30 = 29.6 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium (359 mg/100g) combined with very low potassium (25 mg/100g) and high saturated fat (~9.5 g/100g) from butter-laminated croissant
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this item due to its high sodium content (359 mg/100g), negligible potassium offset (25 mg/100g), and high saturated fat from the butter croissant, all of which compound cardiovascular and blood pressure risk; if consumed, restrict to half a sandwich and request reduced-sodium ham.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined high-GI croissant base (GI ~67) with low dietary fibre (0.81 g/100g) and estimated trans fat (~0.2 g/100g) worsening insulin resistance
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid this item as the butter croissant provides a medium-to-high glycaemic load with minimal fibre (0.81 g/100g) to blunt the glucose response, and the estimated trans fat content further impairs insulin sensitivity; substituting a wholemeal roll would substantially improve the glycaemic and metabolic profile.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (28.46 g/100g), estimated saturated fat (~9.5 g/100g), estimated trans fat (~0.2 g/100g), and high sodium (359 mg/100g) from processed ham and butter croissant
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This item is unsuitable for individuals with cardiovascular disease due to its high saturated fat (~9.5 g/100g) and estimated trans fat (~0.2 g/100g) from the butter-laminated croissant, combined with high sodium from the processed picnic ham, all of which elevate LDL cholesterol, promote arterial inflammation, and raise blood pressure; consumption should be minimised or replaced with a lean protein on wholegrain bread.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Estimated trans fat (~0.2 g/100g) and high saturated fat (~9.5 g/100g) from butter-laminated croissant, plus arachidonic acid from processed ham
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this item as the estimated trans fat (~0.2 g/100g) and high saturated fat from the croissant promote TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory cytokine activity, and the processed ham contributes arachidonic acid which further amplifies joint inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A ham and egg croissant is a Western-style breakfast or lunch sandwich consisting of a buttery, flaky, laminated croissant filled with sliced picnic ham and a fried egg. The croissant is made by layering butter into a yeast-leavened dough through a folding and rolling process, producing a light, airy, crescent-shaped pastry with a golden, crisp exterior and soft, layered interior. The fried egg adds richness and protein, while the picnic ham contributes a mild, salty, savoury flavour. This item is widely available in Singapore school canteens, bakeries, and café-style food stalls, and is commonly consumed as a breakfast or working lunch option. Nutritionally, it is energy-dense at 380 kcal/100g, with high total fat (28.46 g/100g) dominated by saturated fat from the butter croissant (~9.5 g/100g estimated) and a modest estimated trans fat contribution (~0.2 g/100g) from pastry margarine used in some commercial preparations. Carbohydrate content is 22.78 g/100g with very low dietary fibre (0.81 g/100g), resulting in a medium-to-high glycaemic index (~67) and a meaningful glycaemic load per serving. Sodium is 359 mg/100g from the processed ham, with very low potassium (25 mg/100g), making this item clinically concerning for individuals managing hypertension, type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis. It is not suitable for vegetarian, vegan, halal, or kosher diets without modification.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F08020035, confidence 0.95 in source record). Saturated fat and trans fat are estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite data for croissant and ham, clearly documented. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database croissant value, which is the closest approved analogous source.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (9.5 g/100g) and trans fat (0.2 g/100g) are not reported in the HPB source record and have been estimated using USDA FoodData Central data for plain butter croissant (USDA FDC ID 174987) and sliced ham composite, proportioned to the ingredient ratio described. These values are estimates and should be confirmed with laboratory analysis or a more specific HPB entry if available. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, water_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown, moisture, and micronutrient panel data for this specific ham and egg croissant composite are not separately reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 3. GI of 67 is an analogous estimate based on plain croissant GI from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for ham and egg croissant was found in any approved source. GL per 130g serving = 19.8. 4. This food is not a Singaporean dish by cuisine origin (it is Western/French-derived); therefore, the Singaporean multilingual naming requirement (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) does not apply. Local names are set to null accordingly. 5. Halal status is not confirmed as picnic ham is pork-derived; is_halal is set to false.
